metadata.title: Estimates of the current and future burden of cancer attributable to active and passive tobacco smoking in Canada.
metadata.study_type: Journal Article, Research Support, Non-U.S. Gov't
metadata.text: Strategies focused on reducing the prevalence of tobacco smoking are crucial for cancer control in Canada.

metadata.title: Smoking Cessation and Menstrual Cycle Phase
metadata.text: Tobacco use is a risk factor for at least 20 types of cancer and remains the leading preventable cause of cancer in Canada.

metadata.title: Lung cancer and smoking prevalence in Canada.
metadata.text: This article reviews lung cancer mortality and smoking prevalence in Canada.

metadata.title: Estimates of the current and future burden of cancer attributable to active and passive tobacco smoking in Canada.
metadata.study_type: Journal Article, Research Support, Non-U.S. Gov't
metadata.text: In this study, we estimate the proportion of cancer cases in 2015 attributable to past tobacco smoking and passive exposure in Canada and the proportion of cancers in the future that could be prevented through the implementation of interventions targeted at reducing tobacco use.

metadata.title: Analysis of cigarette smoking habits of cancer patients referred to the Northeastern Ontario Regional Cancer Centre.
metadata.text: To review cigarette smoking among patients referred to the Northeastern Ontario Regional Cancer Centre (NEORCC).

metadata.title: Socioeconomic status and lung cancer risk in Canada.
metadata.text: This study examines SES and lung cancer risk in Canada.

metadata.title: Geographical Disparities in Lung Cancer in Canada: A Review.
metadata.study_type: Journal Article, Review, Research Support, Non-U.S. Gov't
metadata.text: While established risk factors for lung cancer exist in Canada, there remains ambiguity regarding the underlying provincial and territorial trends.

metadata.title: Lung cancer survival by immigrant status: a population-based retrospective cohort study in Ontario, Canada.
metadata.text: Lung cancer is one of the most common cancers and causes of cancer death in Canada.

metadata.title: Evaluating the Impact of the McMaster Optimal Aging Portal on Cancer Prevention Behaviours in Underserved Populations
metadata.text: Half of cancers are preventable through lifestyle modification such as smoking cessation, healthy eating, increasing physical activity and reducing alcohol intake; however, few Canadians engage in behaviours that are in line with cancer prevention guidelines.

metadata.title: Burden of lung cancer attributable to occupational diesel engine exhaust exposure in Canada.
metadata.study_type: Journal Article, Research Support, Non-U.S. Gov't
metadata.text: We estimated that 2.4% (95% CI 1.6% to 6.6%) of lung cancers in Canada are attributable to occupational DEE exposure, corresponding to approximately 560 (95% CI 380 to 1570) incident and 460 (95% CI 310 to 1270) fatal lung cancers in 2011.

metadata.title: Lung cancer and smoking prevalence in Canada.
metadata.text: For men, age-adjusted lung cancer death rates are highest in Quebec and the Atlantic provinces, areas that had Canada's highest smoking prevalence twenty years ago.

metadata.title: Geographical Disparities in Lung Cancer in Canada: A Review.
metadata.study_type: Journal Article, Review, Research Support, Non-U.S. Gov't
metadata.text: This review underscores the disparities in lung cancer prevalence and mortality rates between eastern and western Canadian provinces.

metadata.title: Improving Detection of Early Lung Cancer in a Diverse Population (IDEAL) Study
metadata.text: The evidence led to the implementation of LDCT lung cancer screening in Canada for people who have smoked heavily in the past or are still smoking.

metadata.title: Non-Small-Cell Lung Cancer in 2022: A Review for General Practitioners in Oncology.
metadata.text: Lung cancer is the leading cause of cancer death in Canada and a significant cause of morbidity for patients and their loved ones.

metadata.title: Lung Cancer Inequalities in Stage of Diagnosis in Ontario, Canada.
metadata.study_type: Journal Article, Research Support, Non-U.S. Gov't
metadata.text: Lung cancer is the most common cancer and cause of cancer death in Canada, with approximately 50% of cases diagnosed at stage IV.

metadata.title: Estimates of the current and future burden of cancer attributable to active and passive tobacco smoking in Canada.
metadata.study_type: Journal Article, Research Support, Non-U.S. Gov't
metadata.text: We estimated that 17.5% (32,655 cases; 95% CI: 31,253-34,034) of cancers were attributable to active tobacco smoking and 0.8% (1408 cases; 95% CI: 1048-1781) to passive tobacco exposure in never smokers.

metadata.title: Smoking Cessation and Menstrual Cycle Phase
metadata.text: Smoking cessation is an important cancer prevention strategy for the close to 2 million Canadian women who currently smoke.

metadata.title: Active cigarette smoking, variants in carcinogen metabolism genes and breast cancer risk among pre- and postmenopausal women in Ontario, Canada.
metadata.study_type: Journal Article, Research Support, Non-U.S. Gov't
metadata.text: Cigarette smoking is strongly associated with various diseases including many cancers; however, evidence regarding breast cancer risk remains inconclusive with some studies reporting no association, and others an increased risk with long duration and early initiation of smoking.

metadata.title: Cigarette smoking and cancer of the pancreas: evidence from a population-based case-control study in Toronto, Canada.
metadata.text: Results are reported with respect to smoking data from a population-based case-control study of pancreatic cancer conducted in Toronto, Canada, between 1983 and 1986.

metadata.title: Access to Cancer Drugs in Canada.
metadata.text: Lung cancer, because of the multiple subtypes now identifiable and because of the myriad of new and effective therapies, provides fertile ground to highlight issues related to oncology drug access in Canada [...].

metadata.title: Geographical Disparities in Lung Cancer in Canada: A Review.
metadata.study_type: Journal Article, Review, Research Support, Non-U.S. Gov't
metadata.text: However, a noticeable research gap persists, particularly in systematically examining the factors that amplify geographical disparities in lung cancer incidence and mortality rates within Canada.

metadata.title: Circulating Isovalerylcarnitine and Lung Cancer Risk: Evidence from Mendelian Randomization and Prediagnostic Blood Measurements.
metadata.study_type: Journal Article, Research Support, Non-U.S. Gov't, Research Support, N.I.H., Extramural
metadata.text: Tobacco exposure causes 8 of 10 lung cancers, and identifying additional risk factors is challenging due to confounding introduced by smoking in traditional observational studies.

metadata.title: Social determinants of lung cancer incidence in Canada: A 13-year prospective study.
metadata.study_type: Journal Article, Research Support, Non-U.S. Gov't
metadata.text: Because the Canadian Cancer Registry does not contain socioeconomic data, the 1991 Canadian Census Cohort was used to study social determinants of lung cancer risk in the general Canadian population.

metadata.title: Lung cancer in First Nations, Inuit, and Métis peoples in Canada - a scoping review.
metadata.study_type: Journal Article, Scoping Review, Research Support, Non-U.S. Gov't
metadata.text: Lung cancer also affects First Nations, Inuit and Métis peoples significantly in Canada, which deserves further investigation as there is a literature gap on this topic.

metadata.title: "The great debate" 1: Smoking, lung cancer, and cancer epidemiology.
metadata.text: During the first decades of the 20th century the lung cancer death rate increased sharply in developed countries, including Canada.

metadata.title: Analysis of cigarette smoking habits of cancer patients referred to the Northeastern Ontario Regional Cancer Centre.
metadata.text: Smoking rates and total consumption were highest among those patients with cancers arising in the lung, bladder, esophagus and head & neck regions.

metadata.title: Cigarette smoking and lung cancer--relative risk estimates for the major histological types from a pooled analysis of case-control studies.
metadata.study_type: Comparative Study, Journal Article, Multicenter Study, Research Support, N.I.H., Intramural, Research Support, Non-U.S. Gov't
metadata.text: By using one of the largest lung cancer datasets ever assembled, we explored the impact of smoking on risks of the major cell types of lung cancer.

metadata.title: Burden of lung cancer attributable to occupational diesel engine exhaust exposure in Canada.
metadata.study_type: Journal Article, Research Support, Non-U.S. Gov't
metadata.text: This is the first study to quantify the burden of lung cancer attributable to occupational DEE exposure in Canada.

metadata.title: Cigarette smoking and testicular cancer.
metadata.study_type: Journal Article, Research Support, Non-U.S. Gov't
metadata.text: Data were collected between 1995 and 1996 in Ontario, Canada, as part of the Enhanced Cancer Surveillance Study.

metadata.title: Risk Perception Among a Lung Cancer Screening Population.
metadata.study_type: Journal Article, Research Support, Non-U.S. Gov't
metadata.text: This study analyzed data from the Pan-Canadian Early Detection of Lung Cancer (PanCan) Study to address the following questions: Which factors are associated with the perception of lung cancer risk?

metadata.title: Risk of lung cancer in relation to various metrics of smoking history: a case-control study in Montreal.
metadata.text: The purposes of this study are: to provide new data on smoking-lung cancer associations and to explore the relative impact of different dimensions of smoking history on lung cancer risk.

metadata.title: Social determinants of lung cancer incidence in Canada: A 13-year prospective study.
metadata.study_type: Journal Article, Research Support, Non-U.S. Gov't
metadata.text: This study examines incidence rates of lung cancer and histologic subtypes by educational attainment, income and occupation in a broadly representative sample of Canadians aged 25 or older.

metadata.title: Lifetime probability of developing lung cancer, by smoking status, Canada.
metadata.text: When smoking status is not adjusted for, the lifetime risk of developing lung cancer is approximately 96/1,000 and 43/1,000 for males and females respectively.

metadata.title: Cytisine vs Nicotine Replacement Therapy
metadata.text: Although the tobacco use rate has substantially gone down in the general population, significant differences exist between sub-populations in Canada, for example Ottawa's highly vulnerable homeless or at-risk for homelessness population has an almost 100% tobacco smoking rate relative to 9-18% in the rest of the Canadian general population.

metadata.title: Lung Cancer Among Firefighters: Smoking-Adjusted Risk Estimates in a Pooled Analysis of Case-Control Studies.
metadata.text: We used pooled information from the SYNERGY project including 14 case-control studies conducted in Europe, Canada, New Zealand, and China, with lifetime work histories and smoking habits for 14,748 cases of lung cancer and 17,543 controls.

metadata.title: Passive and active smoking and breast cancer risk in Canada, 1994-97.
metadata.study_type: Journal Article, Research Support, Non-U.S. Gov't
metadata.text: Active and passive smoking may be associated with increased breast cancer risk, particularly premenopausal risk.

metadata.title: Provincial Variation in Adherence to Breast Cancer Screening in Canada: Evidence From the Canadian Partnership for Tomorrow's Health.
metadata.text: Breast cancer is the most commonly diagnosed cancer among women in Canada.

metadata.title: Incidence trends in lung and bladder cancers in the Nordic Countries before and after the smoking epidemic.
metadata.study_type: Journal Article, Research Support, Non-U.S. Gov't
metadata.text: The data suggest that the approaching of the incidence rates for lung and bladder cancer can be expected in the course of the abating smoking epidemic.

metadata.title: [Diagnosis of lung cancer].
metadata.text: The most significant risk factor is active smoking, which accounts for over 85% of lung cancer deaths.

metadata.title: Risk of lung cancer in relation to various metrics of smoking history: a case-control study in Montreal.
metadata.text: Irrespective of the histologic type of lung cancer and the smoking metric examined, high levels of smoking led to high levels of risk, for both men and women.

grouped_hits.title: Estimates of the current and future burden of cancer attributable to active and passive tobacco smoking in Canada.
grouped_hits.study_type: Journal Article, Research Support, Non-U.S. Gov't
matched_sentences: Strategies focused on reducing the prevalence of tobacco smoking are crucial for cancer control in Canada.

In this study, we estimate the proportion of cancer cases in 2015 attributable to past tobacco smoking and passive exposure in Canada and the proportion of cancers in the future that could be prevented through the implementation of interventions targeted at reducing tobacco use.

We estimated that 17.5% (32,655 cases; 95% CI: 31,253-34,034) of cancers were attributable to active tobacco smoking and 0.8% (1408 cases; 95% CI: 1048-1781) to passive tobacco exposure in never smokers.

grouped_hits.title: Smoking Cessation and Menstrual Cycle Phase
matched_sentences: Tobacco use is a risk factor for at least 20 types of cancer and remains the leading preventable cause of cancer in Canada.
Smoking cessation is an important cancer prevention strategy for the close to 2 million Canadian women who currently smoke.

grouped_hits.title: Lung cancer and smoking prevalence in Canada.
matched_sentences: This article reviews lung cancer mortality and smoking prevalence in Canada.
For men, age-adjusted lung cancer death rates are highest in Quebec and the Atlantic provinces, areas that had Canada's highest smoking prevalence twenty years ago.

grouped_hits.title: Analysis of cigarette smoking habits of cancer patients referred to the Northeastern Ontario Regional Cancer Centre.
matched_sentences: To review cigarette smoking among patients referred to the Northeastern Ontario Regional Cancer Centre (NEORCC).
Smoking rates and total consumption were highest among those patients with cancers arising in the lung, bladder, esophagus and head & neck regions.

matched_sentences: While established risk factors for lung cancer exist in Canada, there remains ambiguity regarding the underlying provincial and territorial trends.
This review underscores the disparities in lung cancer prevalence and mortality rates between eastern and western Canadian provinces.
However, a noticeable research gap persists, particularly in systematically examining the factors that amplify geographical disparities in lung cancer incidence and mortality rates within Canada.
